title: Topic 402: The Psychology of Military Gaming: War Games and Recruitment
content:
# Topic 402: The Psychology of Military Gaming: War Games and Recruitment

## ABSTRACT

This paper bravely charges into the digital battlefield to investigate the peculiar romance between the United States military and the video game industry. We explore the transition from pixelated fantasies to potential enlistment, examining how the military has masterfully co-opted the very tools of adolescent leisure for its recruitment ambitions. Through a witty yet critical lens, this paper deconstructs the psychological underpinnings of military-themed video games, from the Pavlovian thrill of unlocking a new rifle skin to the carefully cultivated camaraderie of a virtual squad. We will analyze whether pwning noobs in *Call of Duty* truly prepares one for the complexities of modern warfare, or if it's merely a brilliant, if somewhat unsettling, marketing strategy. This paper navigates the murky waters of "militainment," questioning the ethical implications of turning war into a game, and a game into a recruitment pipeline. Ultimately, we seek to understand not just how the military is using games to find its next generation of soldiers, but what this says about our own psychology and the gamification of everything, including conflict itself.

## INTRODUCTION

In the hallowed halls of online gaming, where Cheeto dust and youthful bravado form a potent aerosol, a new kind of warrior is forged. This digital gladiator, fueled by energy drinks and a thirst for virtual glory, can single-handedly capture a flag, defuse a bomb, and rack up a kill streak that would make a seasoned general blush. They are masters of their domain, their thumbs a blur of tactical precision. But does this digital prowess translate to the grit and gravity of real-world combat? The United States military seems to think so, or at least, they're willing to bet a significant portion of their recruitment budget on it.

This paper delves into the fascinating and faintly Orwellian world of military gaming, a realm where the lines between entertainment and enlistment are increasingly blurred. For decades, the military has been more than just a passive observer of the video game industry; it has been an active participant, a collaborator, and, in some cases, a developer. The most notorious example, of course, is *America's Army*, a game literally created by the U.S. Army to give civilians a taste of military life. It was a masterstroke of marketing, a free-to-play first-person shooter that was as much a public relations tool as it was a game. This paper will explore the psychological tactics employed in military-themed video games to attract and prime potential recruits, all while questioning if a high kill/death ratio in a game translates to real-world military prowess. We will dissect the phenomenon of "militainment," a portmanteau that perfectly captures the unholy union of military objectives and entertainment media. Is it a harmless bit of fun, a clever recruitment strategy, or a subtle form of psychological conditioning? Grab your virtual helmets, and let's find out.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, never one to miss an opportunity for some good old-fashioned chin-stroking, has produced a respectable body of research on the curious intersection of video games and the military. The prevailing sentiment, filtered through layers of academic jargon, is that something is definitely afoot. The concept of the "military-entertainment complex," a term that sounds like it was coined by a particularly paranoid sociology professor, has gained significant traction. This theory posits a symbiotic relationship between the military and the entertainment industry, where each feeds the other in a perpetual cycle of mutual benefit. The military gets a powerful platform for shaping public perception and reaching potential recruits, while the entertainment industry gets access to cool, authentic military hardware and expertise to make their products more realistic and, therefore, more marketable.

At the heart of this complex lies the notion of "militainment." Scholars have noted that the lines between war and entertainment have become increasingly blurred, with real-world conflicts broadcast on cable news with the same dramatic flair as a summer blockbuster, and video games simulating combat with breathtaking fidelity. The U.S. military's own foray into game development with *America's Army* is a case in point. As noted on its own Wikipedia page, the game was conceived by Colonel Casey Wardynski as a "strategic communication device" [1]. It was designed to give young Americans a virtual taste of army life, a free sample of the military experience, if you will. The game was, by all accounts, a resounding success, attracting millions of players and, according to the Army's own data, a significant number of whom clicked through to the official recruitment website. Some critics have gone so far as to label it a "propaganda device," a charge the Army has not vehemently denied.

Research has also delved into the psychological effects of playing these games. While the age-old debate about whether violent video games create violent individuals continues to rage, a more nuanced conversation has emerged about the subtler effects of these games. Studies have explored how military-themed games can shape players' attitudes towards the military and warfare, potentially fostering a more militaristic mindset. The concept of "moral disengagement," the psychological process of convincing oneself that ethical standards do not apply in a particular context, has been linked to the playing of military-themed first-person shooters. In the sanitized world of a video game, where enemies are faceless avatars and violence is without consequence, it becomes easier to pull the virtual trigger. The question, of course, is whether this desensitization carries over into the real world.

## ANALYSIS

The psychological wizardry at play in military-themed video games is both subtle and surprisingly effective. It's a masterclass in behavioral psychology, a carefully orchestrated symphony of reinforcement schedules, social influence, and cognitive reframing. Let's dissect this digital frog, shall we?

First, we have the irresistible allure of **operant conditioning**. Video games are, at their core, Skinner boxes for the digital age. Players are rewarded for desired behaviors with a constant stream of positive reinforcement. Kill an enemy, get points. Complete an objective, unlock a new weapon. Level up, and you get a shiny new badge to display next to your username. This system of rewards, of constant, measurable progress, is incredibly addictive. It taps into our fundamental desire for achievement and recognition. The military, with its own rigid hierarchy of ranks and awards, is a real-world analogue of this system. The game, in a sense, is training players to respond to the same kinds of incentives that the military uses to motivate its soldiers. It's a form of pre-training, a gamified introduction to the military's motivational structure.

Next, we have the powerful influence of **social identity theory**. Humans are social creatures, and we have a deep-seated need to belong to a group. Video games, particularly multiplayer games, are brilliant at fostering a sense of camaraderie and shared identity. Players join clans, form squads, and work together to achieve common goals. They develop their own jargon, their own inside jokes, their own unique culture. This virtual brotherhood can be a powerful draw, especially for young people who may be feeling alienated or disconnected in their offline lives. The military, of course, is the ultimate in-group. It offers a powerful sense of belonging, of being part of something larger than oneself. The virtual squads of *Call of Duty* and *Battlefield* are, in a way, a training ground for the real-world squads of the Army and the Marines. The games are selling not just the action and the excitement, but the promise of brotherhood.

Then there is the curious case of **cognitive dissonance**, or the mental discomfort experienced when holding two or more contradictory beliefs. How does a player reconcile the fact that they are having fun participating in a simulation of something as horrific as war? The answer lies in the game's ability to reframe the experience. The violence is sanitized, the enemy is dehumanized, and the moral complexities of war are conveniently swept under the rug. The player is not killing a person; they are eliminating a target. They are not participating in a brutal conflict; they are competing in a challenging game. This reframing allows the player to enjoy the experience without having to confront the grim reality of what it represents. It's a form of psychological self-defense, a way of having your cake and eating it too.

Finally, we have the slow, creeping effect of **desensitization**. Repeated exposure to violence, even simulated violence, can have a numbing effect. The more you see it, the less it affects you. This is not to say that playing video games will turn you into a cold-blooded killer. However, it can lower your emotional response to violence, making it seem more normal, more acceptable. For the military, this is a desirable outcome. A recruit who is already desensitized to the sight of violence is a recruit who is one step closer to being combat-ready. The game is, in a sense, a form of psychological inoculation, a way of preparing the mind for the horrors of war by exposing it to a diluted, gamified version of the real thing.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The U.S. military's love affair with video games is not just a theoretical concept; it's a well-documented reality with a plethora of real-world examples. The most blatant, of course, is the aforementioned *America's Army*. Launched in 2002, this game was a masterclass in soft-power recruitment. It was a high-quality, first-person shooter that was completely free to play. The catch? It was also a meticulously crafted advertisement for the U.S. Army. The game was designed to be as realistic as possible, with a focus on teamwork, strategy, and adherence to the Army's core values. Players had to complete virtual basic training before they could even play online, a clever bit of gamification that mirrored the real-world enlistment process. The game was a massive success, attracting millions of players and serving as a powerful recruitment tool. The Army even set up booths at gaming conventions and esports tournaments, allowing potential recruits to play the game and talk to real soldiers. It was a brilliant, if slightly unsettling, example of the military-entertainment complex in action.

But the military's involvement in the gaming world goes far beyond its own bespoke creations. The military has also been known to collaborate with commercial game developers, lending its expertise and equipment in exchange for a more positive portrayal of the armed forces. The *Call of Duty* franchise, one of the most successful video game franchises of all time, has a long history of working with the military. The games often feature realistic depictions of military hardware and tactics, and the storylines tend to portray the U.S. military in a heroic light. The military has even used modified versions of commercial games for its own training purposes. *VBS2*, a military training simulator, is based on the engine of the popular game *Arma 2*. The line between entertainment and training is becoming increasingly blurred.

The military's presence in the gaming world has not been without controversy. In 2020, the U.S. Army's esports team came under fire for its recruitment practices on the popular streaming platform Twitch. The team was accused of using a fake Xbox controller giveaway to lure viewers to a recruitment page, and of banning users who asked questions about war crimes in the chat. The incident sparked a public outcry and led to a proposed ban on military recruitment on the platform by Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. The Army eventually stopped streaming on Twitch, but the incident served as a stark reminder of the ethical complexities of using video games as a recruitment tool.

## DISCUSSION

The gamification of military recruitment has profound implications for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits. It's a fascinating case study in the power of marketing, the psychology of persuasion, and the changing nature of warfare itself. What can we learn from the military's foray into the world of video games?

First, it highlights the incredible power of **experiential marketing**. The military is not just selling a job; it's selling an experience. By allowing potential recruits to virtually experience what it's like to be a soldier, they are creating a much more powerful and persuasive marketing message than any traditional advertisement could ever hope to achieve. This is a lesson that other industries have taken to heart. Car companies offer virtual test drives, real estate agents offer virtual tours, and clothing brands offer virtual fitting rooms. The military, in a sense, was a pioneer in this field.

Second, it underscores the importance of **community and belonging** in consumer behavior. The military is not just recruiting individuals; it's recruiting them into a community. The camaraderie and sense of shared identity that are fostered in military-themed video games are a powerful draw. This is a lesson that brands have been quick to learn. Companies now spend millions of dollars building online communities around their products, fostering a sense of belonging and brand loyalty. The military's success in this area is a testament to the power of social influence in shaping our choices and behaviors.

Finally, it raises some thorny **ethical questions** about the gamification of serious issues. Is it appropriate to use a medium that is primarily associated with fun and entertainment to recruit people for a job that involves real-world violence and risk? Is it possible to have a truly informed and consensual decision when the recruitment process is so heavily gamified? These are questions that we, as a society, need to grapple with. The gamification of military recruitment is just one example of a much broader trend. We are seeing the gamification of everything, from education to healthcare to personal finance. While gamification can be a powerful tool for engagement and motivation, we need to be mindful of its potential downsides, particularly when it is applied to issues of life and death.

## CONCLUSION

So, what have we learned from our whirlwind tour of the military-entertainment complex? We've learned that the U.S. military is a surprisingly savvy marketer, with a keen understanding of the adolescent psyche. We've learned that video games are more than just a harmless pastime; they are a powerful tool for shaping attitudes, beliefs, and behaviors. And we've learned that the line between entertainment and reality is becoming increasingly blurred, with potentially profound consequences for us all.

Does a high K/D ratio in *Call of Duty* make you a good soldier? Probably not. But it might make you more likely to consider a career where you can put those skills to the test. The gamification of military recruitment is a complex and multifaceted phenomenon, with both positive and negative implications. It's a testament to the power of technology to shape our world in ways we are only just beginning to understand. So the next time you see a teenager glued to their screen, lost in the world of a military-themed video game, don't be so quick to judge. You might just be looking at the future of the U.S. armed forces.

[1] Wikipedia. (2024). *America's Army*. Retrieved from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/America%27s_Army

[2] The Guardian. (2024). *The US military is embedded in the gaming world. Its target: teen recruits*. Retrieved from https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2024/feb/14/us-military-recruiting-video-games-targeting-teenagers

[3] ScienceDirect. (2017). *Use of military-themed first-person shooter games and militarism*. Retrieved from https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563217305617
